questions.description: (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse critically whether the Commission effectively "promote[s] the general interest of the Union" as required by Article 17(1) TEU. Your answer should particularly focus on the Commission's power of initiative in the legislative process, its function in ensuring the application of measures adopted by the Union institutions and its role as 'guardian of the Treaties', as well as referring, where relevant, to case- law of the Union judicature. (B) By reference particularly to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice compare and contrast: (i) the legal nature of a "regulation", a "directive" and a "decision" in Union law; (ii) the core differences between a "legislative act" and a "non-legislative act" in Union law as regards both their nature and the means by which they are adopted; (iii) the difference between "delegated'' acts and "implementing" acts in Union law?

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B).
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) Article 29.4.5° of the Constitution of Ireland permits Ireland to be a Member State of the European Union, while under Article 29.4.6°, no provision thereof "invalidates laws enacted, acts done or measures adopted by the State ... that are necessitated by the obligations of membership of the European Union ...", of which Article 29.4.5° permits Ireland to be a member. Analyse critically the case-law of the High and Supreme Court interpreting the concept of 'necessitated by the obligations of membership', with particular reference to the constitutional limits that case-law has discerned with regard to transposing, or giving further effect to, Union legislation, especially directives, by way of statutory instrument under the European Communities Act 1972, as amended. (B) Discuss critically whether the influence and effect of the majority Supreme Court judgments in Crotty v An Taoiseach [1987] I.R. 713, with regard to when proposed amendments to the Union Treaties may lawfully be effected by Ireland without a constitutional amendment, will be considerably diminished by the majority judgments of the Supreme Court in the recent case of Pringle v The Government of Ireland [2012] IESC 47.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B).
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) "The question of whether a natural or legal person seeking the annulment of an EU act under Article 263 TFEU has established that the contested measure 'directly and individually concerns' the applicant is a difficult question to answer with absolute clarity." Critically assess this statement by reference to relevant case-law, where the Union judicature has considered the elements that a natural or legal person seeking the annulment of an EU act of the Council, the Commission or of the European Central Bank, including 'a regulatory act' as referred to in Article 263 TFEU, fourth subparagraph, needs to establish, in order to demonstrate that such an applicant has satisfied the 'direct and individual concern' locus standi test to which that subparagraph refers. [Or]{.underline} (B) By reference to relevant case-law, critically assess: (i) The different elements of the legal test used by the Court of Justice to determine whether a body, seeking to make a preliminary reference under Article 267 TFEU to the Court of Justice is a 'court or tribunal' for the purposes of Article 267 TFEU; and (ii) (ii) The different situations where the Court of Justice has held that preliminary references to it may be unnecessary or inadmissible.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following nine judgments, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following nine judgments, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature: i) Case C-91192 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR I-1337; ii) Case T-342/99 Airtours v Commission [2002] ECR II-2585; iii) Case C-224/01 Kobler [2003] ECR I-10239; iv) Case T-201104 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601; v) Case T-342/07 Ryanair Holdings v Commission [2010] ECR II-3457; vi) C-236/09 Association beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats and Others v. Council [2011] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of01103/2011); vii) Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 08/03/2011); viii) Case C-34/09 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and The Attorney General [2012] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of27111!2012); ix) Joined Cases C-584/10 P, C-593110 P and C-595/10 P Commission, UK and Council v Kadi ('Kadi II') [2013] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 18/07/2013).

questions.description: Answer both parts (A) Child psychologists have conducted research which…
questions.text: Answer both parts (A) Child psychologists have conducted research which confirms that exposure to indecently dressed dolls can lead some young girls to form negative views about their sexuality. Acting on these findings, the Irish Minister for Children proposes to ban the imported 'Disco-Cutie' female doll from retail sale in the State, on the grounds that the doll wears indecent clothing and is thus an unsuitable role model for encouraging 'moral behaviour' in young girls in the 7 to 9 years age bracket. Disco-Cutie's importers complain that the Minister would be acting in breach of EU law, particularly as 'Rocky', a male wrestler doll which is produced in Ireland, is not being banned from retail sale in the State, even though research confirms that excessive exposure to such 'Rocky'-type dolls can cause some young boys in the 7 to 9 years age group to become violent towards their peers by mimicking Rocky's physically aggressive wrestling exploits. Following consultation, the Minister indicates that she is considering, as an alternative to banning the retail sale of both doll types, to ban television advertising of all children's dolls before 21h00 each evening, on the ground that this will help prevent the commercial exploitation of children. You have been asked by the Attorney General to prepare an opinion for the Minister for Children advising her as to whether banning the retail sale of both the Disco-Cutie' and 'Rocky' dolls would be compatible with EU law and whether the proposed advertising ban would, be compatible with EU law. In your opinion, refer to relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and case-law. (B) Under the (fictional) draft Education Grants Bill 2014, the Irish Minister for Education proposes making the award of university student maintenance grants subject to certain conditions relating, inter alia, to the length of time for which the educational body with which applicants for such grants plan to enroll has been in existence and the place of residence of the applicant. Accordingly, it is proposed that, before a grant would be approved, the educational body with which the student plans to enroll must be capable of demonstrating 10 years' continuous experience in the provision of university- level courses in the State. Furthermore, the grant applicant must be able to demonstrate that s/he has resided in the State or another EU Member State for a minimum of three of the five years immediately preceding the date of the proposed enrolment. You have been asked by the Attorney General to prepare an opinion for the Minister for Education advising him as to whether the proposed provisions would be compatible with EU law. In your opinion, refer to relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and case-law.

questions.description: Hans, Irma and Jorg are all German citizens who have been resident in… Manslaughter, Director.
questions.text: Hans, Irma and Jorg are all German citizens who have been resident in Finland since 2003. Hans came to Finland at the age of 16 with his parents, as his father was posted to work there by his employer. In 2008, Hans left home and moved into a flat. He is currently a final year student at university in Helsinki. He is now estranged from his parents, who have returned to Germany, and has very little income. He has applied for assistance from a Finnish government fund which provides support for final year students, but has been turned down, as he is not a Finnish national. Hans urgently needs funding to allow him to complete his degree. Irma has been working full-time as a graduate beautician for several years in Helsinki. She has recently met, and wishes to marry, Abdullah, a Somali sailor, who has been working on a ship sailing between Helsinki and various African ports. Inna's plan is to move to the UK with Abdullah,.. but she needs to .know whether i) her Finnish diploma, which was awarded after three years study at degree level, will be recognised by the competent British authorities; and ii) it would make any difference to Abdullah's right to reside with her in the United Kingdom if she does not marry him before she moves to the United Kingdom. Jorg is an anaesthetist. He was convicted in 2009 of manslaughter and sentenced to three years' imprisonment. The offence occurred when he left the operating theatre during an operation to make a phone call, and failed to notice that the oxygen supply had become blocked. Jorg has recently been released from prison and wishes to move to Sweden to become the commercial director of a private health clinic. Advise Hans, Irma and Jorg by reference to relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and case-law whether EU law may allow them to achieve their objectives.

questions.description: The (fictional) Directive 2010/333 provides that "all downloads of… Consideration, Directive.
questions.text: The (fictional) Directive 2010/333 provides that "all downloads of copyright material shall be effectively secured by digital rights management techniques (DRM) so as to ensure the protection of the interests of the copyright holder". The aim of DRM is to prevent copyright-protected material from being made available to third parties by physically preventing its being transferred or copied. The Directive should have transposed into national law by 30 November 2012. In Ireland, the Directive was finally implemented by the (fictional) European Communities (Digital Copyright Regulations) 2014, which came into effect on 1 March 2014. The 2014 regulations provide that "all downloads of copyright material made for valuable consideration shall be effectively secured against any adverse impact on the interests of the copyright holder". In the United Kingdom, an almost identical provision has been enacted in the (fictional) Copyright, Designs and Patents (Amendment) Act 2012, which entered into force on 1 December 2012 which implements the Directive in that Member State. Megabooks Plc, a large company established in Ireland but operating principally in the UK, is the copyright holder for a great number of literary works which are available in downloadable form. Megabooks is concerned about its position in relation to downloads by: i) The British Library to its registered subscribers. The British Library is funded by the United Kingdom Department for Culture, Media and Sport and maintains the official collection of material published in the UK, including material for which Megabooks holds the copyright. It provides downloads to subscribers without charge. The downloads are not subjected to DRM but subscribers must give an undertaking to use downloaded material for private study and research and not to make it available to any third party. Megabooks believe that subscribers are in fact making copyright material obtained from the British Library available to third parties in both Ireland and the United Kingdom. ii) Getabook, which is an Irish commercial provider of downloadable digital books, charges customers for each download. It has a licence, entered into in 2005, with Megabooks, which allows it to download digital versions of much of the material for which Megabooks holds the copyright. Getabook used to subject all downloads to DRM but has recently ceased to do so, stating that DRM is no longer cost-effective. Instead, it refuses to supply any customer who has made copy right material obtained from Getabook available to others. Megabooks does not regard this as adequate protection of its interests. iii) Student Geeks was a loose group of law and computer science students who hacked into Megabooks computer system over a number of years and provided downloads of material for which Megabooks holds the copyright to students throughout Ireland. The group stopped operating on 28 February 2014, but Megabooks has identified most of those it regards as responsible and believe they may have received small fees for the service they provided. As a specialist in EU law, you have been asked to advise Megabooks as to any action it could take in Ireland, and/or the United Kingdom, based on its rights under the Directive to protects its interests and/or to seek redress for any infringement(s) of those rights as may have occurred. Consider also whether Megabooks could terminate Getabook's licence and, if it did, if it would thereby expose itself to a potential counterclaim by Getabooks based on a breach of Article 102 TFEU? Prepare an opinion by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law.

questions.description: Answer Part (A), Part (B) OR Part (C).
questions.text: Answer Part (A), Part (B) OR Part (C). Do NOT attempt more than one part. (A) In Westland, a Member State of the European Union, electric power was for the most part generated in solid-fuel fired power plants. The market comprised five major producers of approximately equal size. In 2011, the trade association, West Power of which all five producers are members, took an initiative to phase out generating capacity as the market struggled with over-capacity and low prices. At a meeting of the association, the producers reached agreement on the phasing out of the three production plants, the owners of which were to be compensated. In addition, the producers agreed on the establishment of a consortium that would be devoted to the development of green power i.e. principally wind power, with the result that a joint venture (WestWind) in which the major producers held 20% share each was established. FE1 Power, one of the five producers has engaged you to undertake a competition law audit of its agreements and activities. Assess critically whether the agreement on capacity reductions is contrary to EU competition law and if so, what options, if any, are open to FE1 Power to reduce its resulting potential exposure should the Commission launch an investigation. [OR]{.underline} (B) Discuss the main features of the European Commissions Leniency practice in Article 101 TFEU cartel investigations. Ensure that you support your answer by reference to relevant Commission Decisions. [OR]{.underline} (C) "The notion of advantage has been expressed in a variety of ways, but all are driven by the quest to establish whether the transaction, activity or conduct amounts to a normal commercial transaction or rational market behaviour. Different contexts will demand nuanced tests...." Wyatt and Dashwood, European Union Law (6th ed.2011) at p.853. With reference to Article 107(1) TFEU assess critically the tests which the Commission and the Union judicature respectively have adopted and applied in assessing whether an alleged scheme of aid may be regarded as "favouring certain undertakings or the production of certain goods".

questions.description: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Fundamental Rights.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation from a relatively powerless Assembly under the 1952 ECSC Treaty to the considerably strengthened institution it represents today" (Craig & De Burca, EU Law: Texts, Cases and Materials, (Oxford, OUP, 4th Edition). 2007, p. 58). By reference to, inter alia, Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice analyse this statement with particular reference to: (i) the evolution of the European Parliament's role in the EC and now EU legislative processes up to and including its role as co-legislator under the TEU and TFEU; (ii) the development of the European Parliament's relationship with the European Commission; and (iii) the features that distinguish the European Parliament's legislative role from that of the Oireachtas under the Constitution of Ireland. <!-- --> (B) Discuss, with reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, the origins, development and current scope in EU law of the doctrine of the protection for fundamental rights in EU law. What is the importance of the protection of fundamental rights at EU level for the relationship between EU and national law?

questions.description: "The Treaty of Lisbon has had a profound effect on the legal order of the European Union and on the competences of its…
questions.text: "The Treaty of Lisbon has had a profound effect on the legal order of the European Union and on the competences of its political institutions". Discuss critically this statement with reference to what you regard as the most significant changes introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon. Support your answer by reference to any relevant case-law interpreting those changes.

questions.description: Crafty Builder obtained planning permission to build a wind -farm from Gullible County Council ("GCC"), a local…
questions.text: Crafty Builder obtained planning permission to build a wind -farm from Gullible County Council ("GCC"), a local authority in Westland, a (hypothetical) EU Member State. GCC imposed three substantive conditions in the permission restricting the proposed development to a maximum of 10 turbines, all of which had to be at least 1,000 metres from the nearest dwelling and none of which could be more than 60 metres high. FE-1 Association, a local residents' group, appealed the grant of the planning permission to the Westland Planning Board (''the Board"), citing, inter alia, a failure by GCC to carry out an environmental impact assessment ("BIA") as required by the Directive No. 2011/92/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 December 2011 on the assessment of the effects of certain public and private projects on the environment (O.J. No. L 26, 28. 1.2012, p. 1, ''the EIA Directive"). The Board took the view that, notwithstanding the EIA Directive, it was only bound by Westland planning regulations. FE-1 initiated a judicial review of that decision but, having failed to secure a protective costs order from the Westland High Court protecting its members from an adverse costs order if the judicial review application were unsuccessful, did not pursue its application. The wind farm was then built but 12 turbines were constructed, several of which were over 60 metres high and located within less than 1,000 metres of the nearest dwelling. Crafty Builder applied for retention planning permission on the bases that 12 turbines were necessary to make the project economically viable and that none of the turbines was within 500 metres of any dwelling, which is the minimum specified in national planning guidelines of which Westland planning law permits GCC to take account. GCC decided to grant the retention permission sought. FE-1 understands from one of its members, who is a law student, that the EIA Directive provides for access to justice at reasonable cost and that this provision has not been transposed into Westland law, and that the Westland practice of granting retention or regularisation permissions for projects falling within the scope of mandatory EIA under the EU Environmental Impact Assessment Directive was found to be incompatible with the EIA Directive by the Court of Justice in Case C-215/06 Commission v Ireland (2008] ECR I-491 I. However, it appears that no steps have been taken by Westland to amend its planning laws to limit the grant of retention permissions following the judgment in Case C-2.1 5/06. As a solicitor specialising in EU law, you have been approached by FE-1, which wishes to challenge the grant of the retention permission and to re-enter its earlier challenge to the decision of the Westland Planning Board to uphold the initial grant of retention permission in the case, for relevant EU-law legal advices. In addition, you have been approached for similar EU law advices by some individual members of FE- 1, who wish to bring a claim for damages for the reduction in the value of their properties occasioned by the construction of the wind farm in breach of EU law by the developer and because that construction has, they allege, caused their properties to be more prone to flooding from the local river. Set out your legal advices on: (i) whether and in what circumstances FE-1 and/or the individual applicants could rely on the EIA Directive against, firstly, GCC and the Board and/or, secondly, the developer with regard to: (a) the grant of the initial permission; (b) the grant of the retention permission; and (c) the refusal of the High Court to make a protective costs order; (ii) whether, in what circumstances and against whom potentially the adversely affected property owners might have a right of action for damages based on EU law.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following nine judgments, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following nine judgments, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature: (i) Case 26/22 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; (ii) Case C-50/00 P Union de Pequeños Agricultores (UPA) v. Council [2002] ECR I-6677; (iii) Case C-200/02 Zhu & Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; (iv) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR U-3601; (v) Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 08/03/2011) ; (vi) Case C-370/12 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and The Attorney General [2012] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 27/l 1/2012); (vii) Joined Cases C-274/11 and C-295/11 Spain & Italy v Council [2013] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 16/04/2013); (viii) Joined Cases C-584/10 P, C-593/10 P and C-595/10 P Commission, UK and Council v Kadi ('Kadi II') [2013] ECR I-OOOO· (Judgment of 18/07/2013); (ix) Case C-300/11 ZZ v Secretary of State for the Home Department 2013] ECR I-0000 (Judgment of 04/06/2013).

questions.description: Under Article 19(1) TEU, the Court of Justice of the European Union is tasked with "ensur[ing] that in the…
questions.text: Under Article 19(1) TEU, the Court of Justice of the European Union is tasked with "ensur[ing] that in the interpretation and application of the treaties the law is observed''. By reference to the specific forms of jurisdiction conferred on the Court of Justice of the European Union under Part Six, Title I, Section 5, of the TFEU (comprising Articles 251 to 281 TFEU), and the case-law interpreting that jurisdiction, assess critically whether the Court of Justice of the European Union has successfully carried out this task. In your answer, focus on its jurisdiction under Articles 258, 263 and 267 TFEU.

questions.description: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Regulation.
questions.text: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) Consider whether Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters (''the Brussels I Regulation") establishes clear jurisdictional rules for determining which national courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdictional rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001, which is based on a defendant's domicile, i.e. that '"persons domiciled in a Member State shall, whatever their nationality, be sued in the courts of that Member State", and the special rules prescribed in Article 5(1) and (3) of the Regulation with regard to "matters relating to a contract " and "matters relating to tort, delict or quasi-delict" where under a person may also be sued in the courts, respectively, of ''the place of performance of the obligation in question" and of "the place where the harmful event occurred or may occur", as interpreted in the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice. [Or]{.underline} (B) Under Article 67(4) TFEU ''[t]he Union shall facilitate access to justice, in particular through the principle of mutual recognition of judicial and extrajudicial decisions in civil matters". Assess by reference particularly to Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation") and Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 of 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility ("the Brussels 2 bis Regulation"), as well as the case-law of the Court of Justice interpreting those Regulations, the extent to which you consider the Union is achieving the objective of Article 67(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Answer Part (A), Part (B) [or]{.underline} Part (C).
questions.text: Answer Part (A), Part (B) [or]{.underline} Part (C). Do [not]{.underline} attempt more than [one]{.underline} part. (A) In Utopia, a (hypothetical) EU Member State, most dentists are self-employed or employed with private health-care institutions. In 2005, a law established the 'National Dental Council' ("NDC"), most of whose members were appointed by the trade association, the 'Dental Club'. Most self-employed dentists in Utopia are members of the NDC, as are many dentists in employment. The remaining members of the NDC are appointed by the Utopian Minister for Health. The main statutory task of the NDC is to develop quality standards for dentists, but following a widespread price increase in the cost of dental services in 2012, the Minister for Health asked the NDC to develop common fee-scale recommendations for dental services. The fee scales were not made binding upon the dentists, but were strongly recommended by both the Minister and the NDC. A private clinic, FE1-Fix, refused to participate in the NDC, and lodged a complaint with the European Commission. The Commission initiated an investigation regarding the role of the Utopian Authorities, the NDC and the dentists participating in the NDC. The Commission has issued a press release stating: (i) that, in its opinion, the Utopian Authorities have infringed Article 4(3) TEU and 101 TFEU; and (ii) that the dentists had infringed Article 101 TFEU. The dentists consider that they were not undertakings in the sense of Article 101 TFEU when acting as members of the NDC, that trade between Member States was not affected by their actions as NDC members, that the price discussions were not anti-competitive as the initiative had come about in response to price increases, and that, in any case, they had been compelled by the Utopian authorities to participate in the NDC. As a specialist in EU competition law, you have been engaged by the Utopian Attorney General to prepare, by reference inter alia to relevant case-law, a legal opinion analysing whether: (i) the Utopian authorities' actions amounted to an infringement of EU law, particularly Article 101 TFEU read in combination with Article 4(3) TEU, and, if so, what are the potential consequences of such an infringement; (ii) the dentists had infringed Article 101 TFEU. [Or]{.underline} (B) (i) Explain by reference to relevant case-law the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. <!-- --> (iii) Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, how a dominant position can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU. [Or]{.underline} (C) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position?

questions.description: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) Critically assess the approach taken by the Commission and by the Union judicature concerning the definition of relevant product markets in the application of the Merger Control Regulation (now Council Regulation (EC) No. 139/2004 and formerly Council Regulation (EEC) No. 4064/89). [Or]{.underline} (B) Ireland was exempted under finance legislation from excise duty fossil fuels used in the production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and 2008. In 2008, the exemption was replaced with a reduced rate under the Finance Act of that year. The Commission, which had expressed concerns to the Government regarding the exemption, initiated in 2006 the procedure provided for in Article 108(2) TFEU (formerly Article 88(2) EC) with regard both to the exemption and reduced rate. In its resulting decision in 2010, the Commission has decided that both the exemption and reduced rate comprise State aids that are incompatible with the common market. However, its decision only orders Ireland to recover the difference between the reduced rate and the normal rate of excise duty on fossil fuels from the date of introduction of the reduced rate in 2008. This is justified in the Commission decision because, under a 1992 directive, certain Member States, including Ireland, were authorised to continue to apply until 2008 to certain mineral oils (fossil fuels) existing national exemptions from or reduced rates of excise duty, provided the fuels are used for certain specific purposes, including for the production of alumina as with the Irish exemption for alumina produced in the Shannon region. As a specialist in EU State aid law, the Attorney General has asked you to advise her on whether there might be grounds to challenge the Commission decision before the Union judicature that both the initial exemption and, since 2008, the reduced rate amount to State aid for the purpose of Article l 07(1) TFEU. Set out your advices. In doing so, consider also whether it could be contended that the exemption, as granted by Ireland until 2008, is not an aid that may be attributed to Ireland for the purpose of Article 107(1) TFEU, as the application of the exemption was authorised by the excise duty directive. In your advices, explain the procedure to be followed by Ireland if it were to bring an annulment action and before which court of the Union judicature such an action would be heard at first instance. Consider also if there is a risk that the Commission decision on recovery could be challenged as being too lenient before the EU judicature by competing alumina producers who are not located in the Shannon region. Before which court could such an action be brought in the first instance?

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B).
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of conferral. The use of Union of Union's competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality (Article 5(1) TEU) By reference to relevant case-law, discuss: (i) the principle of conferral and the constitutional significance within the Union legal order of the choice of appropriate legal basis for Union acts; [And]{.underline} (ii) whether the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality have, since their formal introduction by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1993, acted as an effective constraint on the use by the Union of its competences? [OR]{.underline} (B) Article 15 TFEU, which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1st May 1999, provides that: "[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person having its registered office in a member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph". Analyse the extent to which this Treaty provision, EU legislation and the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for Union citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by the institutions of the Union?

questions.description: Answer Part (A) or Part (B).
questions.text: Answer Part (A) or Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) Article 29.4.5° of the Constitution of Ireland permits Ireland to be a Member State of the European Union, while Article 29.4.6° thereof provides that no provision thereof "invalidates laws enacted, acts done or measures adopted by the State ... that are necessitated by the obligations of membership of the European Union..." Analyse critically by reference to relevant High and Supreme Court case-law how this provision has facilitated Irish courts to give full effect and primacy to EU law within the Irish legal order. [Or]{.underline} (B) Analyse critically whether the influence and effect of the majority Supreme Court judgments in Crotty v An Taoiseach: [1987] l.R. 713, with regard to when proposed amendments to the Union Treaties may lawfully be effected by Ireland without a constitutional amendment, has been considerably diminished by the majority judgments of the Supreme Court in the case of Pringle v The Government of Ireland [2012] IESC 47.

questions.description: Answer part (A) or (B).
questions.text: Answer part (A) or (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) One of the novel features of the Community legal order is the power given to the Commission to supervise compliance by the Member States with their obligations under the Treaties." (Audretsch, Supervision in European Community Law (2nd ed., 1986) Appraise the procedures under Articles 258 to 260 TFEU by reference to relevant Court of Justice case-law and from the perspective of their contribution to securing the Member States' compliance with their obligations under EU law. From this perspective, also evaluate the changes to these provisions introduced by successive Treaty reforms, including the Treaty of Lisbon. [Or]{.underline} (B) The preliminary reference procedure under Article 267 TFEU institutionalises a form of cooperation between the Union and the national judiciary. Having regard to the provisions of Article 267 TFEU and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, discuss the respective roles of the Court of Justice and the national courts under the Article 267 TFEU procedure. Your answer should also discuss the extent to which the preliminary reference procedure has permitted the Court of Justice to develop and maintain the uniform interpretation and application of Union law through the Union.

questions.description: Ivan is a Russian national who is married to Jess, a British national.
questions.text: Ivan is a Russian national who is married to Jess, a British national. The couple live in Dublin, where they have established a successful import-export business in the form of a company, into which Ivan has invested substantial amounts of his (significant) personal capital and in which both Ivan and Jess are salaried directors. Politically, Ivan is a close supporter of the Russian Government. The couple is apprehensive that Ivan's political ties and views may result in the EU adopting sanctions against him that could result in the Irish authorities freezing his assets and bank accounts thereby preventing him from continuing as a director of and being employed by the company. The couple is also apprehensive that any such EU sanctions, as well as any further Irish measures taken in implementation thereof, would effectively prevent Jess from continuing to operate the company's business, given the possibility, because of Ivan's shareholding in the company, that its accounts might also be frozen. Accordingly, Ivan and Jess have sought your advice, as a specialist in EU law, regarding whether: (i) they would have a right to be informed and heard before any such sanctions were imposed; (ii) they could challenge, and, if so, before what courts and on what basis, any EU sanctions measures adopted against Ivan and/or the company, as well as further Irish measures adopted in implementation of such EU sanctions measures. Set out your advices by reference to relevant EU Treaty provisions and case-law.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law, and in…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law, and in particular with the fundamental freedoms guaranteed by EU law: (i) A (fictional) tax by Mayo County Council on the transportation of natural gas extracted off the Mayo coast and processed within the Council's functional area out of that area, with the result that only gas extracted and used in County Mayo is not subject to the charge. Mayo County Council says it is justified because local gas users pay rates and other local charges and have to tolerate the location of a massive gas refinery in the county; (ii) A provision in the (fictional) Corrective Lenses Act that authorises the sale of contact lenses solely in shops which specialise in the supply of medical devices, such as opticians. The Department of Health says that the measure is necessary in the interest of protecting human health to ensure that potential purchasers of contact lenses get proper advice regarding the desirability of having their eyes tested first and their suitability for wearing contact lenses; (iii) A provision in the Finance Acts prohibiting residents of the State from using within the State, save on a purely temporary basis, mechanically propelled vehicles that are not registered within the State. The Department of Finance says that this measure is necessary to ensure that State residents pay vehicle registration tax and annual motor tax within the State on vehicles they use on an extended basis within the State.

questions.description: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B).
questions.text: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "Of all social matters addressed by Union law it is the area of sex discrimination and pay that has deepest roots in the Treaties and keenest teeth" (see 'Edward and Lane on European Union Law', 2013, p. 867). By reference to relevant Treaty provisions, directives and case-law, assess the effectiveness of the "teeth" that Union law has developed in its prohibition of discrimination based on sex both within and outside the workplace. Assess in particular the contribution of the case-law of the. Court of Justice to this development and consider whether you would agree that it represents one of the Union's finest social achievements to date. [Or]{.underline} (B) Under Article 67(4) TFEU "[t]he Union shall facilitate access to justice, in particular through the principle of mutual recognition of judicial and extrajudicial decisions in civil matters". Assess by reference particularly to Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation") and Council Regulation (EC) 2201/2003 of 27 November 2003 on the recognition and enforcement of judgments in matrimonial matters and matters of parental responsibility ("the Brussels 2 bis Regulation"), as well as the case-law of the Court of Justice interpreting those Regulations, the extent to which the Union is achieving the objective of Article 67(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Damages.
questions.text: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) The FE-1 Society, a group of concerned law students has complained to the Competition Authority regarding the activities of insurance undertakings operating on the non-life insurance market in Ireland, which the society alleges, are exchanging amongst themselves market sensitive information relevant for determining insurance premiums. On investigation, the Competition Authority has found evidence that the insurance undertakings, which include several subsidiaries and branches of overseas insurance companies, may, in addition to having exchanged such information at several hotel meetings and on the fringes of industry conferences and seminars, have concerted to persuade consumers to purchase general civil liability insurance along with motor vehicle insurance. In particular, with regard to this latter aspect, the Authority has found that virtually all insurers operating on the Irish market include 'third-party plus', a condition which includes general civil and public liability insurance as standard in all motor vehicle policies offered to drivers aged under 25, or otherwise engaged in full-time education. There is also evidence of 'pressure' being exerted on new entrants to the market to adopt similar practices, including evidence of a submission to the Financial Services Regulator by a representative association of insurers calling on that Regulator to refuse to issue new insurance licences to operate on the Irish non-life market to overseas insurance undertakings unless they agreed to participate in what was described in the submission as the "Irish Insurance Information Exchange System, an essential IT tool in the fight against insurance fraud". The Competition Authority estimates that the effect of the information exchange in respect of matters unrelated to the legitimate investigation and detection of fraudulent insurance claims on the non-life market could have been to push up average retail i.e. consumer, premiums by as much on average as 10%. The information exchange appears only to have been directed at retail insurance policies. As an EU competition law specialist, you have been requested to advise regarding: (i) the evidence the Competition Authority would need to establish that an agreement between undertakings, a concerted practice and/or a decision of an association of undertakings in violation of Article 101 TFEU has occurred if it chose to bring an action for declaratory relief before the High Court; [and]{.underline} (ii) whether the FE-1 Society could succeed in bringing individual actions based on Article 101 TFEU for damages against the participating insurance undertakings before the same Court. [Or]{.underline} (B) Discuss the main features of the European Commission's leniency policy in Article 101 TFEU cartel investigations. Ensure that you support your answer by reference to the Commission notice on immunity from fines and reduction of fines in cartel cases and relevant Commission Decisions.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following ten cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following ten cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice and/or General Court: (i) Case 106/77 Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; (ii) Case T-342/99 Airtours v. Commission [2002] ECR 11-2585; (iii) Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR 1-7747; (iv) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v. Commission [2007] ECR 11-3601; (v) C-236/09 Association beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats and Others v. Council [2011] ECR 1-000 (Judgment of 01/03/2011); (vi) Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR 1-0000 (Judgment of 08/03/2011); (vii) Case C-34/09 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and The Attorney General [2012] ECR 1-0000 (Judgment of 27/11/2012); (viii) C-12/11 McDonagh v Ryanair [2013] ECR 1-0000 (Judgment of 31 January2013); (ix) Case C-131/12 Google Spain SL & Google Inc. v. Agencia Espanola de Protecci6n de Datos ((AEPD) & Mario Costeja Gonzalez [2014] ECR 1-0000 (Judgment of 13/05/2014); (x) Joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12 Digital Rights Ireland v. Minister for Communications, Marine and Natural Resources & Ors and Karntner Landesregierung & Ors, [2014] ECR 1-0000 (Judgment of 08/04/2014).
